did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used the InceptionResNetV2 model and a self-developed Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) algorithm to process images from the HAM 10000 database for the classification and differential diagnosis of skin cancer (dermatology task).

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original work involving the development of a facial skin disorder identification system and the application of the InceptionResNetV2 model and a self-developed CNN algorithm to the HAM 10000 database. It describes specific methods, system architecture, and results regarding image processing and classification for educational purposes, which constitutes an original technical study rather than a literature review.

title: AI HAM 10000 Database to Assist Residents in Learning Differential Diagnosis of Skin Cancer
journal: 5th IEEE Eurasian Conference on Educational Innovation 2022
brief_summary: This study developed an AI-assisted educational system to help dermatology residents learn the differential diagnosis of skin cancer using the HAM 10000 database. The system integrates a motorized facial skin disorder identification device with an AI identification sub-system based on the InceptionResNetV2 model and a self-developed CNN algorithm. Residents can capture images of affected areas and upload them to the system to obtain disease probability predictions. These predictions are compared with clinical knowledge to facilitate rapid learning of imaging features outside of office hours. The research aims to accelerate the training process for young residents by providing immediate, data-driven feedback on skin lesion characteristics.

paper_terms: actinic keratosis and intraepithelial carcinoma/Bowen's disease
lesion_types_studied.details: Grouped as a single class in the HAM 10000 database.

all_task_types.target: Seven classes of pigmented skin lesions (actinic keratosis/Bowen's disease, basal cell carcinoma, benign keratosis, dermatofibroma, melanoma, melanocytic nevus, vascular lesions)
all_task_types.details: The study uses the InceptionResNetV2 model and a self-developed CNN algorithm to classify images from the HAM 10000 database into discrete diagnostic categories of skin cancer. The system calculates the probability of specific skin diseases for differential diagnosis purposes.

datasets_used.name: HAM 10000
datasets_used.description: A large collection of multi-source dermatoscopic images of common pigmented skin lesions, built by Tschandl et al. from the Department of Dermatology, Medical University of Vienna, Austria, and Cliff Rosendahl, Queensland, Australia skin cancer clinic. It contains 10,015 dermoscopy images collected over 20 years. Images were stored in PowerPoint files and Excel databases (Australia) or as transparencies digitized via Nikon Coolscan 5000 ED scanner (Austria).
classes_used: actinic keratosis and intraepithelial carcinoma/Bowen's disease (akiec)
vascular lesions (hemangioma, angiokeratoma, pyogenic granuloma, and hemorrhage) (vasc)

use.training: Used as a training set for academic machine learning purposes to establish an AI recognition system.
use.other: Used to calculate disease probabilities via the InceptionResNetV2 model and self-developed CNN algorithm for educational purposes to assist residents in learning differential diagnosis.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. It describes the development of an AI system and the use of the HAM 10000 database for educational purposes without statistical comparison of results.

HAM 10000 Database.akiec_note: Images of solar keratosis were very rare in the HAM 10000 database with only 315 cases, and the disease site was not clearly marked, which weakened the clinical significance of AI identification.

HAM 10000.notes: The paper describes the development of an AI-assisted educational system using the InceptionResNetV2 model and a self-developed CNN algorithm on the HAM 10000 database. It does not report quantitative performance metrics (such as accuracy, precision, recall, or F1-score) for the classification task. The primary result reported is the observation that actinic keratosis (akiec) was underrepresented in the dataset (315 cases), which limited the clinical significance of the AI identification for that specific class.

architecture_details.description: The system uses the InceptionResNetV2 architecture provided by Google along with a self-developed CNN algorithm to redefine pixels and calculate disease probabilities from the HAM 10000 database images.

training_details.purpose: Academic machine learning to establish an AI recognition system for differential diagnosis education
training_details.details: The paper does not report specific training hyperparameters, optimizer, loss function, or training schedule. It mentions using the database as a training set for academic purposes.

classes: actinic keratosis and intraepithelial carcinoma/Bowen's disease (akiec)
vascular lesions (hemangioma, angiokeratoma, pyogenic granuloma, and hemorrhage) (vasc)

preprocessing_techniques: Manual cropping of scanned images to 800x600 pixels at the center of the lesion
Manual histogram correction to enhance visual contrast and color reproduction
Digitization of transparencies using Nikon Coolscan 5000 ED scanner with two scans using Digital ICE
Storage of files as JPEG images with 8-bit color depth at 300 DPI
conclusions: The developed AI-assisted educational system, utilizing the InceptionResNetV2 model and a self-developed CNN algorithm on the HAM 10000 database, helps young dermatology residents learn the imaging features of skin cancer outside of office hours.

The system allows residents to immediately capture photos of affected areas, upload them to the AI program, and obtain disease probability predictions to compare with clinical knowledge, thereby rapidly accumulating experience in differential diagnosis.

The study identified a limitation in the HAM 10000 database where images of solar keratosis (actinic keratosis) were very rare (only 315 cases) and disease sites were not clearly marked, which weakened the clinical significance of AI identification for this specific class.

Developing a subsidized teaching system for AI imaging is considered of great help to resident education, enabling them to face unknown dermatological diseases and make correct differential diagnoses in the future.
important_remarks: The paper does not report quantitative performance metrics (e.g., accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score) for the AI classification model; it only describes the system architecture and educational utility.

The HAM 10000 database exhibits significant class imbalance, with actinic keratosis (akiec) being severely underrepresented (only 315 cases), which the authors note weakens the clinical significance of AI identification for this specific class.
The HAM 10000 dataset lacks clear disease site marking for some lesions, further limiting its clinical applicability for precise localization tasks.
The study is limited to a proof-of-concept educational system for residents and lacks external validation on independent clinical datasets.
The AI system relies on the InceptionResNetV2 model and a self-developed CNN algorithm, but specific training hyperparameters, loss functions, or optimization details are not provided.

The hardware component (motorized facial skin disorder identification device) is described conceptually but lacks technical specifications regarding image capture quality or standardization compared to clinical dermoscopy.
